full_response:
We work with the hypothetical space-time whose line element is  

ds² = gµν dxµ dxν = –dt² + α² t² dx² + dy² + dz² ,        (1)

where α has dimensions 1/length² so that ds² has dimensions (length)² (we set c = 1).

1.  Proper (invariant) length of the rod at t = –1 s  
The rod lies along the x–axis, its ends are at x = –x₀ and x = +x₀, and we want the spatial distance between the two simultaneous events  

A : (t = –1 , x = –x₀ , y = 0 , z = 0) ,  
B : (t = –1 , x = +x₀ , y = 0 , z = 0) .

For simultaneous events dt = 0 and for our rod dy = dz = 0, so (1) reduces to  

ds² = α² t² dx² .                                             (2)

The spatial metric component is therefore  

gxx(t) = α² t²   →   √gxx = |α t| .

The invariant length is the integral of √gxx along the rod:

L(t) = ∫_{–x₀}^{+x₀} |α t| dx = |α t| (2x₀).         (3)

At t = –1 s, |t| = 1, so

L(–1 s) = 2 α x₀.                                        (4)

2.  How the length changes with time if the ends stay at x = ±x₀  
Using (3) for an arbitrary time t,

L(t) = 2 α |t| x₀.                                      (5)

Thus, while the coordinate positions of the ends are fixed, the proper length is not: it shrinks linearly to zero as t → 0, then grows again for t > 0.

3.  Why this does not violate the “invariance” of the interval  
The word “invariant” means that the value of ds² for a given pair of events is the same in every coordinate system.  In eq. (5) we are not looking at the same two events as time passes; we are comparing different pairs of simultaneous events on the world-tube of each end of the rod.  Because the metric coefficients themselves depend on t, the proper distance between two fixed-coordinate world lines naturally changes with t.  All observers—whatever their state of motion—will agree that the distance between the ends at a given coordinate time t is 2α|t|x₀, so no contradiction arises.

\boxed{L(t=-1\ \text{s}) = 2\,\alpha\,x_0,\qquad
       L(t) = 2\,\alpha\,|t|\,x_0\ \text{for fixed}\ x=\pm x_0} 

Confidence: 86%
